sentences.text: Into working within the houses?
My older sister left '37, 1937.
We still by the north, old house.
And '39 we needed to move to Tokaj.
I start right-- I couldn't-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about '38, 1938.
And that was help for my parents, too, because earn some money.
Because my middle sister, the third one, was helping in the house, and cooking, and you know-- the house fraulein, they called it-- [GERMAN].
And my younger sister went to the high school.
And when we moved to-- my mother was so sick she don't want to look for an apartment.
I went with my father to look for the apartment to move from there because--
Because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] those days, when they have some loan in your house-- somehow, you know, five children-- and my father wrote some [NON-ENGLISH], and people doesn't paid.
So they was every house.
So the bank got the house without we know it.
And my two older sisters sended the money to the bank.
But meanwhile came the Jewish law.
Even one, they, I think, owned the bank.
And I went-- I was I think 18 or something. 19-- to the office in Budapest, and I asked them when my father was first war-- how come they don't giving living there?
So somebody, a Gentile person, got the house.
So my older sister of my mother, she lived with us.
And she said, I'm not going to go out in this house alive.
She died.
First time I saw a dead person in the house.
So we went to the city.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we get back our-- they take away our license the tavern, because the big-- and then, a couple of months later, they give it back.
What I show it to you, in the picture [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And they wasn't such a big business.
So Elizabeth and my mother, you know, could handle it.
We were just a couple months we have it.
And I went to-- how I told you-- in different houses.
Meanwhile, I get engaged.
Yeah-- you know, because in the big house was the tavern.
But we needed to move out, so we went to the city.
This was a regular apartment.
We needed to cut out the window for a door.
We needed to inspected, then the regulation, and we get to apply again to get back the license.
It was a very reward-- I think, especially my mother then.
Because her father owned that, too.
Then we got it back.
Already, my father had went the labor camp near to Tokaj, just a couple of months.
That was 1940 already.
Leaved.
Yeah.
I tell you, my uncle from [NON-ENGLISH], they came visit us.
I cannot even remember.
I was in high school, and my younger sister-- five years younger from me-- and he came to [NON-ENGLISH], and he said to my father, you have five daughters.
She had-- all my cousin in New Jersey, you know.
Then why don't you come?
At least I should-- they sponsored the girls.
And my two sisters was at that high school, working in the office.
Different-- one is in the lawyer, one-- and getting very bad situation financially.
So when he went home, my uncle, and he wrote again.
Than if at least the two older sisters should come out.
So the two sisters get the affidavit.
And they couldn't come together because they are not-- while they're allowed.
Margit came-- May 1st arrived to New York or New Jersey, and my older sister in August.
And I supposed to come '38.
Because they wrote to us, they wish my hand should be with them.
Then they go in the easier time.
You know, without the English knowledge, that's a difficulty.
You know-- when we came, it was a difference.
I tell you, that was a big moment.
I told you, we lived in the neighborhood, and we was very good friends.
And when Margit came, about three nice wagon with horse, and the neighbors came and uncles, and my father and my mother couldn't.
She couldn't take it to say goodbye.
But when my older sister came, [LAUGHING] that's really interesting-- her boyfriend, or friend of hers, sent 50 carnations.
And my younger sister, and me, and my father went, and sitting and holding the flowers.
My younger sister almost got sick from the smell-- because it was about 6 kilometer, you know, the other city where they went.
Pardon me?
Well, always have nice friend, naturally.
But in 1940, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '39, I think 1940.
I-- even if I couldn't teach officially-- I-- beside, I went to the houses.
I taught my younger sister and my cousin in the house while I went to the people's houses.
But I went to Budapest, the capital city in Hungary, to see a fashion show, and needed to have my date fixed-- I don't remember.
So anyhow, and I met somebody.
And a couple weeks, a couple maybe-- he came down here.
Engaged in, I think, 1940. 1940-- that somewhere, or January.
Something like that.
His name?
[SIGHS] Gorstein Undorg.
[CRYING] [INAUDIBLE].
The reason I waited for him five years is because [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1941, we supposed to get married or something.
Then his cousin, who was in the Budapest, they get to go to the labor camp.
So he came down, and he was the best man.
And the next city was the wedding.
And people-- I tell you, people, they get married just for Jewish, sometimes, or just for the city hall.
And people said when we was there, then-- why don't you go to the [NON-ENGLISH] and get married?
And he said, I don't want just one night.
So I thankful for that, because I could get pregnant, and I'm not his today.
And they said to-- going to be six weeks.
So we had a date when we're going, but he never came back.
He died in [NON-ENGLISH], or something.
His mother got the notice then.
They don't say they died.
But the whole group had to write-- I don't know how.
So when we came back from the Holocaust-- I know Morton before the Holocaust.
A good friend.
But they were more religious than I was.
I liked to dance, and you know.
And my younger sister-- I told you the three of us came back.
And her boyfriend came back from also a concentration-- I mean, a labor camp-- and they get married.
They ask me if it's all right.
But I said, I want to-- there in 1946.
January 1st-- he asked me to marry.
And I told, if not coming back, I want to be married to you.
Morton was a very respectable man, or boy, in our town.
Everybody liked him.
I respected him, too.
Some people getting them-- you know, a small town, they were to go out then why we not getting married, or what?
Because Ilona is waiting.
While it's-- if she's coming back, have [INAUDIBLE].
But even when we get married and then to Budapest, my fiance's mother was welcome.
Morton and the children.
When we came back the concentration camp, we went to her house.
We don't want to separate them.
In Budapest, doesn't have such a big apartment houses like in Tokaj.
And in Tokaj everybody had a house not apartment.
And she gived her apartment to us.
And she went to stay with her daughter-in-law, the other one.
And then we three of us.
Because when I came home, I don't have the-- you know.
And a cousin of my husband fixed it, so we stayed in Budapest.
Naturally, for his wife, I designed some dress and sewed the dress.
You know, we don't have no money.
We couldn't believe it, you know?
We couldn't believe it. '39-- then going to have so terrible things.
Some people like from Poland and all over the Hungary they came to find a place in Hungary.
And they told the terrible story, but we couldn't believe it.
People.
You know, we never saw them.
I cannot really remember.
I cannot really remember.
How I said, like for instance, in [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, I just told my student the other day, when they said, [NON-ENGLISH].
I said, I lived in-- and my girlfriend wasn't Jewish.
And it was a natural things.
And then the day before Christmas, we went to help decorate the Christmas tree.
Not the Thanksgiving time, but the day before.
It never occurred to me why I don't have it.
Never.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother-- because from the town-- I'm talking about the old house, you know.
From the-- not town, the farm.
They came the midnight mass to Tokaj.
Because in the farm wasn't the church.
And was a special noodle, what my mother made.
Because they stopped in our house and leaved the horse and buggy.
And my mother give the hot meal.
And they came to the window, the children or young adults.
And knocked on the window if we can praise the God.
Naturally, was the answer.
My family.
[SOBBING] My parents and my aunt.
I had an aunt, who they said, they youngest on my father I look like.
And I was very close to her.
You know, we came home to Tokaj, and not one relative.
During the war or?
I told you, we needed to wear a yellow.
Oh, they took away the tavern because the Jewish law came.
Which year?
That's a good question.
I think [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '41 or-- because just a couple months we-- we have it back.
And really cannot exactly remember.
And oh, when there was time when the soldiers, they already-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] we don't have the tavern.
And German people, you know, German soldiers, came through the town.
And they have to-- the Jewish, most of the time-- they put officers and things to the Jewish people.
We had only two bedroom, and we called three, and the kitchen, and everything.
So two German officers-- they don't ask you.
Even if you had just two in your room, one room you needed to give.
And in the wall was my father-- medal, you know, what he get.
And my father spoke fluently in German.
We was lucky.
These two German soldiers was a decent man.
Next door had two old girls.
They get also, too, but they was terrible.
They don't-- they-- I don't even want to tell you what the horrible things-- you know-- did for them.
You know, to don't go to the bedroom, but in the front of the window.
Ours was very nice.
He-- they even came to the kitchen.
My mother used to bake some squash.
You know, we don't have too many things to eat.
And we asked them if you want to eat.
And somehow, they respect us.
And they moved on, you know.
They stay, I don't know, months or a couple of weeks.
I cannot remember.
But that's also happened.
Because we talked to them.
And they was crying, you know.
And they needed to clean their dirty boots.
For us, doesn't.
Pardon me?
I think was '42 or '43.
I don't know.
I really don't remember.
They don't happen.
It was old maids so they don't rape them, or something like that.
But you know, they do the dirt in the house, and-- you know.
Just we got lucky they was nice.
So that's the reason I can't-- I cannot say I hate Germans, what they did for us.
Because they was human being between them, too.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And you said that you wore the Jewish star?
Yes.
I think '43.
I cannot remember.
I think '43.
It doesn't matter.
You see, the Star of David and with yellow needed.
So we embroidered it.
They came the drummer, you know, then all the Jews had to put the yellow star on it.
And I went with the yellow star to the mayor's house to design her daughter's wedding gown.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I arrived in the [NON-ENGLISH], called the coat, and they was solider.
But they was very nice.
But in the other hand, when the Hungarian solider marched, when they was singing songs-- the dirty Jews, you know.
And they have many song when you came there.
And you have lying under your pyres, and this kind of song.
This is-- and the year before, they take the hat off the head and they say, kiss the hand, and nice, and-- You know, many people was one day like you, and next day was enemy.
What can I say?
We needed to work for the everyday living.
And just we couldn't believe it then going to happen.
Then take us to the ghetto, and the temple, and this.
I told you in the morning.
You know something?
I cannot remember.
I just cannot remember.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Funny things.
Many things you remember.
Maybe middle of the night, I will.
That you cannot-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] if I can remember, they used to have Jewish banker or maybe wholesale [INAUDIBLE].
But then they have a Gentile name-- his name-- and was the silent partner or something like that.
If I could remember.
But when my father came back the labor camp, because he was already old.
You know, 64, 65.
Then when the German give back [NON-ENGLISH], the part of Czechoslovakia, then in Tokaj, I have the grape mountain vineyard.
And somebody have in Kosher something.
So he-- they respected him.
And he arranged that and changing.
Like a real estate, he saw-- have someone.
Saturday, I visited my older sister and she give me a letter what we wrote in 1940.
My parents, my mother wrote in it, my father, and three of us sister, even me.
To thank to my sister in America.
They sended 100 [NON-ENGLISH] or dollar, I don't remember-- to help us. 1940 was [? so bad ?] already.
My-- when my father's brother said, you should, you know, go.
My mother said, I won't hear it.
I should learn a new language?
Just couldn't believe it then, this serious thing is going to happen.
Nobody believed that.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Pardon me?
I don't know.
I really don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Many was-- you know, you don't get a new license, or you can't-- you know, more Gentile people open up the general store and other store.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Yeah.
I tell you, our neighbor with horse and wagon helped us.
And could you imagine, for a five room big house?
And had separate summer kitchen and a bath for [INAUDIBLE], and the little-- and go into a two-bedroom [INAUDIBLE], you know-- and kitchen, and a [NON-ENGLISH], and a tiny little yard.
However, it was a barn and, one car, we could have it.
It was first time, unbelievable.
For instance, we had room-- in the kitchen I had my bed and my sewing machine.
I don't know how they were fitting so many things.
Under the bed was a wooden basket.
And when the boys came to visit, I pull out the basket and they sit on it.
And they have a stove not here-- but a wooden, not electric.
And there was a little stool my mother used to sit.
And was a table and my father went it.
And the other table when I can cut dresses.
So, in the two room with my parents and my other two sisters.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
